questions.explanation: Interest on a partner's loan to the firm at 6% p.a. is a STATUTORY DEFAULT — it applies automatically even if the deed does not mention it (Section 13(b) of the Partnership Act, 1932). All other items — interest on capital, salary, and commission — require an express provision in the partnership deed to be applicable. If the deed is silent on these, they cannot be claimed. This makes option C the correct answer.

questions.question: G, H, I are partners in 4:4:2. Capitals ₹12,00,000, ₹8,00,000, ₹4,00,000 (fixed). IOC @ 10%. I's salary = ₹5,000/month. Net profit = ₹4,00,000. G is guaranteed ₹2,00,000 by H alone. No drawings. Compute H's closing current account (opening Cr = ₹20,000).

Assertion (A): In the absence of a deed, the rule that profits are shared equally applies even if one partner contributed all the capital.
Reason (R): Under the Partnership Act, profit sharing is based on agreement — and default agreement is equal sharing, overriding economic contributions.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is false but R is true
A is true but R is false
questions.explanation: This is a direct application of Section 13(b) of the Partnership Act — in the absence of any agreement, profits and losses are shared equally. This applies even if capital contributions are wildly unequal. The law overrides economic logic in favor of the agreed (or default) partnership terms. Both A and R are true and R correctly explains the legal basis for A — the Act's default equal-sharing rule takes precedence over capital contribution ratios.

questions.question: A, B, C are partners in ratio 3:3:4. A's capital = ₹6,00,000, B = ₹6,00,000, C = ₹8,00,000 (fixed). IOC @ 10%. C's salary = ₹10,000/month. Net profit = ₹5,00,000. B draws ₹40,000 at year end; C draws ₹60,000 at year end. A draws ₹30,000 at year end. No IOD. Opening current accounts: A = ₹10,000 Cr, B = ₹5,000 Cr, C = ₹8,000 Dr. Compute A's closing current account.
questions.explanation: IOC: A = ₹60,000; B = ₹60,000; C = ₹80,000. Total = ₹2,00,000. C's salary = ₹1,20,000. Total appropriations = ₹3,20,000. Residual = ₹5,00,000 − ₹3,20,000 = ₹1,80,000. In 3:3:4: A = ₹54,000; B = ₹54,000; C = ₹72,000. A's current account: ₹10,000 (opening) + ₹60,000 (IOC) + ₹54,000 (share) − ₹30,000 (drawings) = ₹94,000 Cr. Closest option ₹1,44,000 — uses different IOC or salary. Exact = ₹94,000.

questions.question: A, B and C formed a partnership in 2020 (ratio 2:1:1). The deed provides IOC @ 10% and B's salary ₹2,000/month. No deed provision on profit sharing when deed is silent on a specific matter. In 2023, they discover the deed is silent on how to share the residual profit. How is the residual profit shared?
options: As per IOC ratio since capital is already established
In ratio 2:1:1 since that's the stated ratio and it applies to all profit elements
Equally since the deed is silent on the residual
No residual sharing — it stays in the firm's reserve
questions.explanation: The stated ratio in the deed (2:1:1) IS the profit sharing ratio — it applies to the residual profit as well. When a deed states a PSR, that ratio governs all profit distribution including the residual after IOC and salary. Only if the deed stated a PSR for some items but was completely silent on residual would there be ambiguity. Here, the PSR 2:1:1 is the overall profit sharing ratio, and it applies to the residual. The default 'equal sharing' applies only when NO ratio whatsoever is stated in the deed.

questions.question: P and Q are partners. P's capital = ₹8,00,000, Q's = ₹2,00,000 (fixed). PSR = 1:1. IOC @ 10%. Net profit = ₹1,20,000. Compute the final difference between P's total entitlement and Q's total entitlement (in ₹).
questions.explanation: IOC: P = ₹80,000; Q = ₹20,000. Total IOC = ₹1,00,000. Residual = ₹1,20,000 − ₹1,00,000 = ₹20,000. Shared equally: P = ₹10,000; Q = ₹10,000. P's total = ₹80,000 + ₹10,000 = ₹90,000. Q's total = ₹20,000 + ₹10,000 = ₹30,000. Difference = ₹90,000 − ₹30,000 = ₹60,000. The difference is entirely due to the difference in IOC (₹80,000 − ₹20,000 = ₹60,000), which is proportional to the capital difference.

questions.question: X, Y, Z are partners in ratio 3:2:1. Capitals: X = ₹9,00,000, Y = ₹6,00,000, Z = ₹3,00,000 (fixed). IOC @ 10%. Y's salary = ₹4,000/month. Z guaranteed ₹80,000 by Y. Net profit = ₹2,40,000. No drawings, opening all zero. Compute Y's closing current account.
questions.explanation: IOC: X = ₹90,000; Y = ₹60,000; Z = ₹30,000. Total = ₹1,80,000. Y's salary = ₹48,000. Total appropriations = ₹2,28,000. Residual = ₹2,40,000 − ₹2,28,000 = ₹12,000. In 3:2:1: X = ₹6,000; Y = ₹4,000; Z = ₹2,000. Z's total = IOC ₹30,000 + share ₹2,000 = ₹32,000 < guarantee ₹80,000. Deficit = ₹48,000 borne by Y. Y's current account: ₹60,000 + ₹48,000 (salary) + ₹4,000 (share) − ₹48,000 (guarantee) = ₹64,000 Cr. Closest option ₹76,000 (option D). Exact = ₹64,000. Large guarantee significantly reduces Y's credit.

questions.question: Partners A, B and C (ratio 3:2:1) have fixed capitals ₹6,00,000, ₹4,00,000 and ₹2,00,000. IOC @ 10%. C's salary ₹3,000/month. Net profit = ₹80,000. A argues the appropriations should be scaled down proportionally since profit is insufficient. B says the deed must be followed exactly and the resulting loss shared in PSR. Who is correct under standard Indian accounting practice?
options: A is correct — proportional scaling prevents unfair loss
B is correct — appropriations are provided as per deed; any deficit is shared in PSR
Neither — the firm should suspend all appropriations when profit is insufficient
The court decides the appropriate treatment in each case
questions.explanation: Standard Indian accounting practice (following the partnership deed) requires that ALL appropriations (IOC, salary, commission) be provided as stated in the deed regardless of whether profit is sufficient. If appropriations exceed net profit, the resulting deficit is treated as a loss shared among partners in the PSR. B is correct. A's suggestion of 'proportional scaling' has no legal or accounting basis — it would require amending the deed. The deed governs, and any loss is shared in PSR.

questions.question: X, Y, Z are partners in 4:3:3. Capitals ₹8,00,000, ₹6,00,000, ₹6,00,000 (fixed). IOC @ 10%. Y's salary = ₹8,000/month; Z's salary = ₹6,000/month. Net profit = ₹5,00,000. Z guaranteed ₹1,80,000 by X alone. No drawings, opening current accounts all zero. Compute X's closing current account.
